Verse 3.14.608.translation: Here the causality of two 'iva'-meanings is cognized; the member is joined with one, the affix with the other.
Verse 3.14.608.commentary: The anatomy of 'kakataliya'. Dvayor iva-arthayor atra nimittatvam pratiyate: the *causality* of *two* *'iva'-meanings* is cognized here — two likenesses, not one. Ekena avayavo yuktah pratyayo 'nyena yujyate: the *member* is joined with *one*, the *affix* with the *other*. So the compound carries a double comparison: one of its members answers to one likeness and the affix to another. This is unlike anything else in the samuddesa — a compound with two comparisons inside it — and 3.14.609 unpacks them.

Verse 3.14.609.translation: Caitra's arriving there is like the crow's arriving; but the bandit's falling upon him is like the palm-fruit's falling.
Verse 3.14.609.commentary: The two likenesses of 3.14.608, and the image is exact. Caitrasya tatra agamanam kakasya agamanam yatha: *Caitra's arriving* there is *like* the *crow's arriving* — he came for his own reasons, as the crow did. Dasyor abhinipatas tu talasya patanam yatha: but the *bandit's falling upon him* is like the *palm-fruit's falling* — the fruit was ripe and would have dropped anyway. So neither event is for the other's sake, and their meeting is the whole of the misfortune. 'Kakataliya' compounds two independent likenesses and means their coincidence — which no member of it says.

Verse 3.14.610.translation: In their conjunction, that other action which arises there — killing and the rest, in the comparandum-meaning — by that the prescription of 'cha' is accepted.
Verse 3.14.610.commentary: Sannipate tayor ya anya kriya tatra upajayate: in their *conjunction*, that *other* action which *arises* there — sannipata: the falling-together; and the third action, which neither party performed. Vadhadir upameye 'rthe: *killing* and the rest, in the *comparandum-meaning*. Taya cha-vidhir isyate: *by that* the *prescription of 'cha'* is *accepted*. So what licenses the affix is the action that came out of the meeting and belonged to neither — the killing that nobody did. Upajayate: it *arises*. This is the finest thing in the last stretch: the compound is licensed by an emergent event, and a coincidence is precisely an event with no agent.

Verse 3.14.611.translation: The substance-word stands in the inherent action; thus the words 'crow' and 'palm' stand for the arriving and the falling.
Verse 3.14.611.commentary: Kriyayam samavetayam dravya-sabdo 'vatisthate: the *substance-word* stands in the *inherent action* — the noun lodges in the action that inheres in what it names. Pata-agamanayoh kaka-tala-sabdau tatha sthitau: the words *'kaka'* and *'tala'* stand for the *falling* and the *arriving*. So 'crow' in the compound means the crow's coming and 'palm' means the fruit's dropping: two nouns, and each has become an event. This is 3.14.529's madhura and matr all over again — the word names the action at its seat, not the seat — and it is why 'kakataliya' can compound two happenings out of two things.

Verse 3.14.612.translation: The sentence which is expository is constructed in this way; the sentence of usage in the world is not employed in this way.
Verse 3.14.612.commentary: Yad anvakhyayakam vakyam tad evam parikalpyate: the sentence which is *expository* — anvakhyayaka, the grammarian's paraphrase (3.14.71's anvakhyanaya) — is *constructed* in this way: parikalpyate, built. Prayoga-vakyam yal loke tad evam na prayujyate: the *sentence of usage* in the *world* is *not employed* in this way. So everything 3.14.608-611 has just unpacked belongs to the exposition and to nothing anyone says. Nobody utters 'as the crow's arriving and the palm-fruit's falling'; they say 'kakataliyam', and they mean coincidence. The analysis is a construction and the word is a word — 3.14.49's atyanta-bheda, held to the very end.

Verse 3.14.613.translation: Of those two whose meeting is seen to be unreasoned, like the crow and the palm-fruit — the derivation of the compound-base is accepted.
Verse 3.14.613.commentary: Yayor atarkita praptir drsyate kaka-talavat: of those two whose *meeting* is seen to be *atarkita* — *unreasoned*, not to be argued out, arriving without a why — *like the crow and the palm-fruit*. Tayoh samasa-prakrter vrttir abhyupagamyate: of *those* the *derivation* of the *compound-base* is *accepted*. So the rule's condition is *atarkita prapti*: an unreasoned meeting. This is one of the loveliest phrases in the samuddesa and one of the most precise: a coincidence is exactly a conjunction for which no reasoning is available, and Sanskrit grammar has an affix for it. Drsyate: it is *seen* to be unreasoned — one sees the absence of a reason, and that seeing is what the compound reports.

Verse 3.14.614.translation: As the crow's killing by the palm-fruit — but whose killing is by a bandit; there, when the other comparandum is made striking, 'cha' is accepted.
Verse 3.14.614.commentary: Kakasya talena yatha vadho yasya tu dasyuna: as the *crow's* killing *by the palm-fruit* — but *whose* killing is *by a bandit*: Caitra's. Tatra citrikrte 'nyasminn upameye cha isyate: *there*, when the *other* comparandum is *made striking*, *'cha'* is *accepted*. Citrikrta: made vivid, made a picture of, rendered arresting. So the affix comes when the compared event has been made *striking* — and that is an aesthetic condition, not a semantic one. Bhartrhari has been describing a figure of speech all along, and here he says what figures are for: 'kakataliya' is used because it makes the thing vivid, and the grammar accommodates the vividness.

Verse 3.14.615.translation: And others: 'cañcatka', of the flickering kind, and 'brhatka' — they describe the gem, the frog and the firefly by similarity.
Verse 3.14.615.commentary: Cancat-prakaras cancatko brhatka iti ca apare: *'cancatka'*, of the *flickering kind*, and *'brhatka'* — the great-ish one — and *others* say. Mani-manduka-khadyotan sadrsyena pracaksate: they describe the *gem*, the *frog* and the *firefly* *by similarity*. So three small pulsing things, named by a mode-affix, and each of them likened to something. The set is unexpected and Bhartrhari has chosen it: a jewel, a frog, a firefly — things that throb. 3.14.616 says what each is likened to, and the verse is a small poem.

Verse 3.14.616.translation: There the firefly is compared by its opening and closing; the frog by its succession of breaths; the gem, its light throbbing.
Verse 3.14.616.commentary: One of the most beautiful verses in the kanda, and it is doing analytic work. Tatra unmesa-nimesabhyam khadyota upamiyate: the *firefly* is compared by its *opening and closing* — unmesa-nimesa: the eye's opening and shutting, the standard Sanskrit measure of an instant. Svasa-prabandhair mandukah: the *frog* by its *succession of breaths* — prabandha: an unbroken series; the throat going in and out. Spandamana-prabho manih: the *gem*, its *light throbbing* — spandamana: pulsing, quivering. So the three are alike in *pulsing*, and each is compared by its own kind of pulse. What the mode-affix catches is a rhythm — and 3.14.321's pravrtti said that a thing's general character *is* a rhythm: manifestation, disappearance, persistence. The firefly is that doctrine made visible.

Verse 3.14.617.translation: That which, though small, is apprehended as great, its light expanding — there, in that very gem, the word 'brhatka' is employed.
Verse 3.14.617.commentary: Pravikasi-prabho 'lpo 'pi mahan ya upalabhyate: that which, *though small*, is *apprehended as great*, its *light expanding* — pravikasin: unfolding, blooming outward. A little stone that throws a great blaze. Brhatka iti tatra eva manau sabdah prayujyate: *there*, in that very *gem*, the word *'brhatka'* is *employed*. So the affix says: great, in the way this small thing is great. It records the discrepancy between the size and the apprehension — and it is the apprehension that the word takes (3.14.569's the object comes about in conformity with the cognition). A jewel is brhatka because of how one sees it.

Verse 3.14.618.translation: By some, 'mode' everywhere is accepted as just similarity; by others, the designation 'mode' is accepted even in difference.
Verse 3.14.618.commentary: Sadrsyam eva sarvatra prakarah kaiscid isyate: by *some*, *'prakara'* — mode, kind, manner — *everywhere* is accepted as *just similarity*: to be of a kind is to be like. Bhede 'pi tu prakarakhya kaiscid abhyupagamyate: by *others*, the designation *'prakara'* is accepted *even in difference*: a mode may be a mode without any likeness. So the last doctrinal question of the samuddesa: is a kind a resemblance? Two answers, both held, neither adjudicated — the work's manner from first to last (3.14.97's the upayas are unsettled among persons).

Verse 3.14.619.translation: Some word expressing a mode stands in what possesses the mode; some, having operated in the mode alone, passes beyond it.
Verse 3.14.619.commentary: Prakara-vacanah kascit prakaravati samsthitah: *some* word expressing a *mode* stands in what *possesses* the mode — it lodges in the thing that has the manner. Prakara-matre vartitva kascid tad vyativartate: *some*, having operated in the *mode alone*, *passes beyond* it — vyativartate: goes past, overshoots. So mode-words do two things: some settle on the possessor, some touch the mode and travel on. The pair is 3.14.354-355's two permutations once more, and it is the last time the samuddesa runs its most characteristic move — set two readings side by side, change one word, and let the reader see that nothing decides.

Verse 3.14.620-621.translation: (620) The taking-up of 'similarity' in the sutra is an indication of what is similar; in the avyayibhava of two equals, the word 'saha' is the expresser. (621) That derivation of what expresses the sense of 'as' — in repetition and in similarity: and this difference in the avyayibhava has been shown by way of difference.
Verse 3.14.620-621.commentary: (620): sadrsya-grahanam sutre sadrsasya upalaksanam — the taking-up of *'sadrsya'* in the *sutra* is an *indication* of what is *similar*: the rule's word points at the similar thing and does not describe similarity (3.14.170's upalaksana). Tulyayor avyayibhave saha-sabdo 'bhidhayakah: in the *avyayibhava* of *two equals*, the word *'saha'* is the *expresser* — a vacaka, where 'sadrsya' was only a pointer. (621): vipsa-sadrsyayor vrttir ya yatharthabhidhayinah — the *derivation* of what expresses the sense of *'yatha'*, in *repetition* (vipsa: 'gramam gramam', village by village) and in *similarity*. Sa ca ayam avyayibhave bhedo bhedena darsitah: and this *difference* in the avyayibhava *has been shown by way of difference* — darsitah, exhibited (3.14.573's the sutra shows the difference by way of difference). So the rule exhibits as a real distinction what is a distinction of the exposition. The samuddesa's last word about rules is the one it began with.

Verse 3.14.622.translation: By some, similarity in 'anu' is accepted as fitness; but that similarity which resides in bodily form is expressed by 'saha'.
Verse 3.14.622.commentary: Sadrsyam yogyata kaiscid anav abhyupagamyate: by *some*, *similarity* in *'anu'* is accepted as *yogyata* — *fitness*, aptness, the capacity to go together (2.317's yogyata, one of the sentence's meaning-conditions). So 'anu' marks not likeness but suitability. Yat tu murti-gatam samyam tat sahena abhidhiyate: but that *similarity* which *resides in bodily form* is expressed by *'saha'* — murti: the shaped body (3.14.485's murti-bheda, which jati- and guna-words did not intend). So the division: 'anu' takes fitness, 'saha' takes bodily likeness. Two particles, two kinds of togetherness, and the difference is whether shape is in question.

Verse 3.14.623.translation: Even in 'being-thus', similarity is grounded in a state of the intellect; where mere difference is grasped, another thing altogether is expressed.
Verse 3.14.623.commentary: The work's thesis, sounded once more before the end. Itthambhave 'pi sadrsyam buddhy-avastha-nibandhanam: even in *itthambhava* — being-thus, the condition of being in a certain way — *similarity* is *grounded in a state of the intellect*: buddhy-avastha-nibandhana, the exact words of 3.14.567 and 3.14.570. Grahane bheda-matrasya tatra anya eva abhidhiyate: where *mere difference* is *grasped*, *another thing altogether* is *expressed*. So even here, at the last technical question, the answer is the one the samuddesa reached at its summit: the likeness is in the mind's condition, and where the mind grasps only difference, something else is being said. Api — *even* — carries it: there is no case left where this does not hold.

Verse 3.14.624.translation: 'The Vahika is an ox' — in the duality, similarity is cited as a counter-instance; when 'white' and the rest is present and accomplished, it is said twice with 'Vahika'.
Verse 3.14.624.commentary: The last verse of the Vakyapadiya, and Bhartrhari ends on the metaphor every Sanskrit grammarian ends on. Gaur vahika iti dvitve sadrsyam pratyudahrtam: *'gaur vahikah'* — *the Vahika is an ox* — the Bhasya's own example, the countryman called an ox for his dullness. In the *duality*, *similarity* is cited as a *pratyudaharana* — a *counter*-instance: the case brought to show what the rule does *not* cover. Suklad au sati nispanne bahikena dvir ucyate: when *'white'* and the rest is *present and accomplished*, it is said *twice* with *'Vahika'*. So: 'the white Vahika is an ox' — and 'Vahika' is said twice over, once for the man and once inside the metaphor, because the qualifier has fixed him and the ox is still there. The two do not merge and they do not part. It is the right ending. The work opened at 1.1 with the Word-Brahman, without beginning or end, undivided, appearing as many; and it closes on a man who is an ox, where a metaphor is a duality that will not resolve into one thing or two — 3.14.367's vyatikirna, intermingled past separating. And the last thing the samuddesa does is call it a counter-instance: the case the rule does not reach. Bhartrhari has spent six hundred verses showing that the science stands very far off (3.14.74), that the divisions are the intellect's (3.14.570), that the upayas must not be taken for true (3.14.76) — and he ends by pointing at an ordinary insult that his grammar cannot derive, and stopping there.
